title: Chapter 7: The Ranger's Compass
content: I ran.

Not the way you run when you're late, or when you're exercising, or when someone calls your name from across a field. The way you run when your body has made a decision your mind hasn't caught up to yet. Feet striking earth, arms pumping, lungs dragging air in great ragged gasps that sounded like they belonged to someone else. I didn't think about direction. I didn't think at all. I just went.

The fire behind me — my fire, the fire I'd made — threw orange light across the yard for a moment, and then the trees swallowed it, and there was only the dark and the fog and the sound of my own stupid breathing.
My hands were still blue.

I could see it in the gaps between the birches — that faint, sick luminescence clinging to my fingers like phosphorescence on lake water. Smoke curled between them, thin and sinuous, not rising but drifting, as if it too was looking for somewhere to go. I tucked them under my arms and kept running.

The fog tried to stop me. It always did. It rolled in thick and white across the forest floor, ankle-deep at first, then knee-deep, then waist-high, pressing its damp fingers against me like it wanted to hold me in place. I pushed through. The Rage Fire was still there — banked, not burning, but present. A coal in my chest that pulsed with every heartbeat, and every heartbeat said *go go go*.
I didn't stop until the trees changed.

It happened gradually, the way important things always do in forests. The young birches and scrub pines thinned out, replaced by something older. Oaks with trunks wider than the Blackwood kitchen table. Elms whose branches interlocked overhead like the vaulted ceiling of a cathedral. And here — finally — the fog couldn't follow. It pooled at the edges of the old growth like water against a dam, white and restless, but it didn't enter. These trees had been here before the fog. They'd be here after it. They didn't have to let it in.

I found an oak at the edge of a small clearing and sat down against it. Hard. The bark was rough through the back of my shirt, every ridge and furrow pressing into my spine, and I was grateful for it. Something real. Something that didn't care about the Buena curse or Rage Fire or how many Aurelias had sat in this same spot and burned. The earth was cool beneath me, damp and loamy, and I pressed my palms flat against it, feeling the cold seep into my skin. My hands were still glowing. Faintly now. The blue had dimmed to a whisper.

The wind moved through the branches overhead, a long slow exhalation that sounded almost like a sigh. Somewhere far away — miles, maybe, out on the water — a foghorn sounded its low mournful note. And beneath it all, my own breathing: ragged, uneven, the breathing of someone who has just done something they can't take back and isn't sure yet whether it was the right thing or the worst thing.

I sat there for a long time. I don't know how long. Long enough for the blue to fade from my fingertips. Long enough for the trembling to move from my hands to my shoulders to somewhere deeper, a shaking that wasn't physical anymore, that was just me, the core of me, vibrating at some frequency I couldn't name.
The Rage Fire waited. It was patient. It had always been patient.
I didn't hear him arrive. That's the thing about Leo Hartwell — he doesn't arrive. He's just there, like a tree that grew in the time you weren't looking.

He sat on a fallen log about ten feet away, one leg pulled up, the other dangling, a piece of pale wood in one hand and a small knife in the other. Whittling. The blade caught what little light filtered through the canopy, flashing in small precise motions. *Shhhk. Shhhk. Shhhk.* Curling ribbons of wood fell at his feet.

He didn't look up. He didn't ask what was wrong. He didn't stare at my hands, though I saw his eyes flick to them once — briefly, without alarm, the way you'd note the weather — and then return to his whittling.
I sat very still. The Rage Fire banked a little lower, not because I was suppressing it, but because there was nothing for it to react to. No demand. No expectation. No one pulling at me.

Minutes passed. The wind moved through the oak above me, and the foghorn sounded again, and Leo kept whittling, and I kept sitting, and neither of us said anything, and it was the most comfortable I'd been in the presence of another person in weeks.
"You smell like smoke," he said finally. Not looking up.
"Yeah."
"The good kind or the bad kind?"

I almost laughed. The sound caught in my throat — not a laugh, not quite, but the shape of one, the ghost of one, something that remembered what laughter felt like and was trying it on again after a long time in storage. It was the first near-laugh since Julian had arrived with his canvases and his questions and his beautiful, demanding eyes.
Leo glanced up at that almost-laugh. Just a glance. Then back to his work.
"Well?" he said.
"I don't know yet," I said. And that was the honest truth.
He nodded, as if this were a perfectly reasonable answer, and went back to whittling. *Shhhk. Shhhk.*

A while later — ten minutes, twenty, I wasn't counting — he stopped. He blew the wood dust off whatever he'd been making, examined it with one eye closed, and seemed satisfied. Then he stood, crossed the distance between us, and held it out to me.
It was a comb.

Wooden. Hand-carved. The teeth were slightly uneven — the middle ones a hair longer than the outer ones — and the back was decorated with a simple pattern of notched lines that could have been decorative or could have been incidental, the idle marks of a knife that didn't know what else to do with itself. It was plain. It was practical. It smelled like fresh-cut cedar.
"For the tangles," he said. "The forest always fixes mine."

That was it. No preamble. No *I made this for you, Aurelia, because I've been thinking about you, because your hair was wild the first time I saw you and I wanted to tame it with my own hands.* None of that. Just: for the tangles. Like it was obvious. Like giving someone a comb was the most natural thing in the world.
I took it.

The wood was warm from his hands. I turned it over once, feeling the grain, and then I raised it to my hair — matted and snarled and half-coming-undone from its braid, the way it always was after the Rage Fire — and I drew it through.
It caught. Of course it caught. I worked it gently, the way you have to with tangles — no forcing, no yanking, just patience and a steady hand. And as I combed, something happened.
My hands stopped glowing.

Not the way they stopped when I suppressed it — that was like clenching a fist, like holding a door shut against something that wanted through. This was different. The Rage Fire just... banked. Settled. Not because I'd pushed it down but because, for one moment, I didn't have to carry it. Someone had given me something to do with my hands that wasn't burning. Someone had offered a small, practical solution to a small, practical problem, and the smallness of it was the whole point.
Julian gave me a portrait. A rendering. Himself, reflected back at me through the lens of his wanting. It was gorgeous. It was suffocating.
Leo gave me a comb.

I combed my hair until the tangles were out, and my hands were just hands, and the coal in my chest had dimsmed to an ember, and I thought: *This is what it feels like. Not being fixed. Being given the space to fix yourself.*
"Thanks," I said.
Leo was already sitting again, starting on a new piece of wood. "The cedar helps," he said. "Repels moths."
I almost laughed again. This time it was closer.
"Come on," he said, standing and pocketing his knife. "I want to show you something."

I followed him deeper into the old growth. The forest changed as we walked — the oaks gave way to trees I didn't have names for, their bark silver-grey and their leaves dark and glossy, almost black in the filtered light. Moss carpeted the ground, thick and springy underfoot, and the air smelled of cedar — from the comb, maybe, or from the trees themselves — and something older beneath it, something mineral and deep, like the smell of rain on stone.

Leo walked ahead of me at an easy pace, not waiting up, not slowing down, just moving at his own rhythm and trusting me to match it. He didn't talk. He didn't fill the silence the way Julian did — with observations and questions and the constant low hum of attention that was flattering until it wasn't. Leo's silence was different. It was the silence of someone who was comfortable in the world and didn't need to prove it.
The trees opened into a clearing, and there they were.
The Boundary Stones.

A circle of seven — no, eight — no. I counted again. Seven visible, and one half-buried in the earth, its top barely breaking the surface like the dorsal fin of something enormous and sleeping. They were grey, the color of old bone, and smooth — not the rough granite of the New England coast but something finer, denser, almost polished. They stood about waist-high, each one roughly the same size, spaced evenly around the perimeter of the clearing. No runes. No markings. Just stones, standing in a circle, older than anything I'd ever touched.

"Nobody knows what they're for," Leo said, standing at the edge of the clearing with his hands in his pockets. He said it the way he said everything — flat, factual, without drama. "The town records don't mention them. The indigenous histories describe this area but not the stones specifically. They're just... here."

"But the trees," I said. I'd noticed it immediately. The trees around the clearing weren't like the trees in the rest of the forest. They were taller. Straighter. Their trunks rose without the usual gnarl and twist of old growth, clean and vertical, and their canopy formed an almost perfect dome overhead, letting in a circle of pale light that fell directly on the stones.
"Yeah," Leo said. "The trees grow differently around them. Straighter. Older. Whatever the stones are doing, the trees like it."
I stepped into the circle.

The air was different here. Still. Not stagnant — clean. Like the air at the top of a mountain, or the first breath after being underwater. I walked the perimeter slowly, trailing my fingers along the stones as I went. Cool. Smooth. The mineral smell was stronger here, sharp and clean.
When I touched the third stone, it happened.
Six heartbeats.
Not mine. Not Leo's. Six distinct pulses, each one distinct, each one alive.

The first was slow and steady, a deep thrum like a drum at the bottom of a well. It paced itself with the patience of something that had been waiting a very long time and could wait longer. Maren. I didn't know how I knew the name, but I did.

The second was rapid and fierce, a stuttering percussion that crackled against my palm like sparks from a fire. Iris. The name came with a flash of something red — not anger, not exactly. Passion. Hunger. A fire that refused to go out.

The third was precise and measured, each beat identical to the one before it, a metronome carved into the stone. Cordelia. The name arrived with the sensation of cool linen and straight lines and a hand adjusting a painting on a wall until it was perfectly level.

The other three I couldn't name — not yet. They were murkier, further away, like voices heard through walls. But they were there. Six heartbeats. Six presences. Six something-elses, living in the stones, waiting in the stones.
The ancestresses.

I pulled my hand back. The heartbeats stopped. The clearing was just a clearing again, and the stones were just stones, and the light was just light, and I was standing there with my hand raised and my heart slamming against my ribs and no way to explain any of it to anyone.
I turned. Leo was watching me from the edge of the clearing, his face unreadable. Not confused. Not concerned. Just present.
"You felt something," he said. Not a question.
I nodded.
He didn't ask what. He didn't move closer. He just stood there, solid and unhurried, his hands still in his pockets, and he gave me the space to decide whether to say it or not.
I decided not. Not yet. Not because I didn't trust him, but because I didn't have the words, and Leo was the kind of person who would wait until I found them rather than supply his own.
"Will you come back?" he asked.
Not *are you okay.* Not *what happened.* Not *what did you feel.* Just: will you come back. A question that assumed I would make my own choice and that my choice mattered.
"Yes," I said.
I meant it.
The walk home was different.

The fog was still there — it was always there, in Blackwood Vale, circling the house like an animal, pressing its damp face against the windows — but it parted around me now. Not dramatically. Not the way it does in movies, splitting open in a grand reveal. Just... stepping aside. A foot of clearance on either side, as if something about me — the ember in my chest, the memory of six heartbeats, the smell of cedar on my hands — made it think twice about getting close.
Or maybe it was afraid of what I carried.
I walked through the fog and it opened before me and closed behind me and I felt, for the first time in days, like I was moving through the world instead of being moved by it.
The house was dark when I got back. Julian's studio light was off. Elinor's too. I climbed the stairs to my room, lit the single lamp on my desk, and opened my journal.
The comb was still in my pocket. I set it on the desk beside the inkwell, its uneven teeth catching the lamplight. Simple. Practical. Real.
I wrote:
*The ranger gave me a comb. The artist gave me a portrait. One is for getting tangles out. The other is for putting them in. I know which one I needed today.*
*I don't know which one I'll choose tomorrow.*

I looked at the words for a long time. Then I closed the journal, turned off the lamp, and sat in the dark with my hand pressed flat against the desk, feeling the grain of the wood, waiting for the six heartbeats to come back.
They didn't. Not here. They were in the forest, in the stones, in the place where the trees grew straight and old and the fog couldn't follow.
But I would go back. I had said so, and I had meant it, and that — the saying and the meaning — was the first choice I'd made since the curse began that felt like it belonged to me.
The ember waited. Somewhere in the stones, six hearts beat on.
